The exhaustive_search_summary demonstrates a comprehensive search across multiple FamilySearch record types (Oregon marriage indexes, death indexes, censuses, draft registrations, newspapers) with explicit notation of negative results. The narrative identifies and resolves a genuine conflict: the FamilySearch index title erroneously lists 'Tossie Bain' as the bride when in fact 'Tossie Bain' is the groom's mother, not his wife; the agent correctly extracted the actual bride ('Rosemarie Graham') from the underlying GedcomX record. Corroboration is strong and independent: the 1962 Oregon Marriage Record (primary derivative) is separately corroborated by the 1991 Oregon Death Index (secondary derivative) listing 'Rosema Taylor' as surviving spouse, and the agent reasonably identifies 'Rosema' as an index truncation of 'Rosemarie,' linking the two sources across 29 years. The declared tier of 'probable' (rather than 'proved') is appropriately justified: the agent notes that derivative indexes rather than original certificates were examined, and that the marriage rests on two sources separated in time rather than two independent original documents from the marriage event itself. The reasoning is transparent and the conclusion is proportionate to the evidence presented.

entry.rationale: Oregon County Marriages 1851-1975 (collection 1803968) is the largest indexed Oregon marriage collection with images. Benny resided in Tillamook County from the 1930s and would most likely have married there. Covers the primary window when he would have married (roughly age 18 onward). Direct evidence of all Oregon marriages through 1975.

Oregon Death Index 1903-1998 (collection 1946790) is indexed and free. Benny died 9 July 1991 in Tillamook, Oregon. Oregon death records from this period typically name the surviving spouse or record marital status; the underlying death certificate (collection 3657650 or 2352707) will identify the wife present at death.

Oregon Marriage Index 1849-1884; 1991-2006 (collection 3500591) fills the post-1975 gap not covered by the County Marriages collection. If Benny married or remarried after 1975, this index (plus its images) would capture it. Critical for the completeness requirement of this question.

The 1940 US Federal Census shows household composition and relationship of each member to head of household. Benny was 18 in April 1940; if he had married by then, his wife would appear in the household. Also establishes his residence in Garibaldi, Tillamook County, confirming the locality for marriage searches.

The 1950 US Federal Census (now public) captures Benny at age 28-29 — old enough to be married with a household. Lists spouse by name with age. Corroborates the identity of any wife found in marriage records and may reveal children's names and ages, suggesting timing of marriages.

Oregon WWII Draft Registration Cards 1940-1945 (collection 2803937) covers men born between 1877 and 1897 (first draft, 1940) and the 'old man' draft (1942, men born 1877-1897). Wait — Benny was born 1921, so he falls under the WWII selective service registrations. The draft card records his contact person (often wife or parent), address, employer, and physical description. A wife named as next of kin would be direct corroborating evidence.

Oregon Tillamook County Records 1854-1967 (collection 2115693) contains 64,546 browse-only images with no indexed records. May include local marriage registers, court records, and other county documents not captured by the statewide indexed collections. Use volume_search to identify relevant image groups if indexed searches leave gaps.

Oregon Marriage Indexes 1906-2009 (Ancestry collection 5193) covers the entire period of interest with index and images. Paid fallback if FamilySearch indexed searches miss Benny, particularly useful for variant name spellings (Ben, Benjamin) or if records were filed under a different county.

Benny died 9 July 1991 in Tillamook. A local newspaper obituary (Tillamook Headlight-Herald or Garibaldi area papers) typically names the surviving spouse, children, and sometimes prior marriages. The GFO Oregon Obituaries index and Newspapers.com/Oregon coverage are the access points. Corroborates the identity of the wife named on the death certificate.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Marriages of Benny Raphael Taylor (1921–1991): Research Argument

**Conclusion (Probable):** Benny Raphael Taylor (FamilySearch: KNJ1-TRX) married at least four times. Three wives are identified by name with documentary support from independent sources: (2) Jolene Eleanor Bartchy, married 23 November 1941, Tillamook, Oregon; (3) Hildagard Bernadine Duclos, married 25 February 1945, Vancouver, Washington; and (4) Rosemarie Graham, married 28 September 1962, Oregon, who survived him at his death on 9 July 1991. A fourth, earlier wife predeceased him before April 1940; her name and the marriage record remain unidentified despite searches of available Oregon and FamilySearch marriage indexes.

---

### The Subject

Benny Raphael Taylor was born 19 July 1921 in McCammon, Bannock, Idaho and died 9 July 1991 in Tillamook, Oregon. He resided in Garibaldi, Tillamook County, Oregon from the late 1920s into at least 1979. His FamilySearch tree identifier is KNJ1-TRX.

---

### Marriage #1: Unnamed Wife (Deceased Before April 1940)

The 1940 United States Federal Census, an original enumeration conducted in April 1940, recorded Benny ("Bennie") Taylor residing in the parental household of Jay Taylor and Tassy Taylor at East Garibaldi Election Precinct, Tillamook, Oregon, with marital status **"Widowed"** (assertion a_024; src_003 — "United States Census, 1940," FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:1:VRTZ-125; original record, primary information about contemporary marital status, direct evidence of widowhood). This single source is sufficient to prove that a prior marriage existed and that the wife died before April 1940.

Searches of the Oregon County Marriage indexes for 1851–1975 (FamilySearch collection 1803968; log_001, negative) and the Oregon Marriage Index 1991–2006 (FamilySearch collection 3500591; log_003, negative) returned no record of this first marriage. The wife’s name remains unknown. This gap is acknowledged as a documented research limitation and does not affect the conclusions about the three subsequent, named marriages.

---

### Marriage #2: Jolene Eleanor Bartchy (1941)

The pre-existing research tree for KNJ1-TRX contains a Couple relationship (tree R1) sourced to an Oregon marriage record documenting Benny Taylor’s marriage to **Jolene Eleanor Bartchy** on **23 November 1941** in Tillamook, Tillamook, Oregon (tree source linked to Oregon Marriage Records, 1906–1968; tree relationship R1).

This marriage is independently corroborated by the Oregon World War II Draft Registration Card for Benny Rapheal Taylor, an original government record completed and signed by the registrant on **16 February 1942** at Garibaldi, Tillamook, Oregon (assertion a_011; src_002 — "Oregon, World War II Draft Registration Cards, 1940–1945," FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:1:QLGY-1BRJ; original record, primary information, direct evidence of named relative/contact). In the field for the person who will always know the registrant’s address, Benny personally named **"Jolene Taylor"** — a surname matching his own, consistent with a wife bearing the married surname. The registration was completed approximately eleven weeks after the November 1941 marriage, reinforcing the inference that the named contact is his wife (assertion a_012; same source, indirect evidence of marital relationship, primary information from the registrant himself).

Jolene Eleanor Bartchy (tree: LL9V-56G) was born 10 November 1926 in Tillamook, Oregon, and died 24 March 2009 in Battle Ground, Clark, Washington. Because she outlived Benny, the marriage ended in divorce before Benny’s next documented marriage in February 1945.

---

### Marriage #3: Hildagard Bernadine Duclos (1945)

Multiple Washington County, Oregon marriage record citations in the pre-existing research tree document the marriage of **Benny R. Taylor** and **Hildegard B. Bauer/Baner/Duclos** on **25 February 1945** in Vancouver, Clark County, Washington (tree sources SBZ6-3B7, SBZ6-37N, SBZ6-79N, SBZ6-S8M, SBZ6-7P5, SL4F-5F6, S2QB-HMP, S2QB-3G2; tree relationship R2). The variant spellings of the bride’s surname across multiple index entries ("Bauer," "Baner," "Dorlos Bauer") reflect phonetic transcription of her birth surname "Duclos" by different recorders.

This marriage is independently corroborated by the 1950 United States Federal Census, an original enumeration conducted on **11 April 1950**, which lists **Benny R Taylor** (head, born 1922 Idaho, occupation Cab Driver) with **Hilda B Taylor** (wife, born 1918 Idaho) in a Portland, Multnomah, Oregon household (assertions a_036, a_037, a_038, a_039; src_004 — "United States, Census, 1950," FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:2:HSJ8-417R; original record, primary information about contemporary residence and household composition, direct evidence of marriage). "Hilda B Taylor" is confidently identified as Hildagard Bernadine Duclos (tree: L7BB-SP1), born 17 August 1917 in Ferdinand, Idaho — the census birth year of 1918 reflects standard one-year rounding from stated age. Five children appear in the 1950 household (Roy D, Jacque F, Barbara M, Ronald J, Mark A Taylor), consistent with the couple having married in 1945.

The 1950 census also lists Benny’s own marital status as "Single" (assertion a_032). This is a clear enumerator or indexing transcription error: the same schedule simultaneously records a Couple relationship between Benny and Hilda B. Taylor, identifying her as his wife. The internal evidence of the record resolves this apparent inconsistency; no external conflict exists.

Hildagard Bernadine Duclos (tree: L7BB-SP1) died 3 April 1998 in Clarkston, Asotin, Washington — she outlived Benny. The marriage therefore ended in divorce before Benny’s next documented marriage in September 1962.

---

### Marriage #4: Rosemarie Graham (1962 — Surviving Spouse at 1991 Death)

**a. The Marriage Record and a Critical Indexing Error**

The Oregon Marriage Records 1906–1968 (FamilySearch collection 3326746) contains an entry for the marriage of **Benny Rapheal Taylor** (born 1921) and **Rosemarie Graham** (born 1942, Portland, Oregon; parents Arthur L. Graham and Rosamund Hoover) on **28 September 1962** in Oregon (assertion a_069; src_005 — "Oregon, Marriage Records, 1906–1968," FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:1:WBF8-1Q3Z; derivative digital index of original Oregon county marriage register, primary information about the marriage event, direct evidence of the marriage).

The FamilySearch index titles for this source (tree sources QDLH-77Y, QDLH-Q9L) describe the entry as “Entry for Benny Rapheal Taylor and **Tossie Bain**, 28 Sep 1962.” This is an indexing misattribution. Examination of the record’s underlying GedcomX persona data shows that “Tossie Bain” occupies a **ParentChild** relationship as the parent of the groom — not a Couple relationship as the bride. "Tossie Bain" is the nickname and maiden-surname variant of Benny’s own mother, **Autossie Ann Bair** (tree: KWZ8-3RD), who died 24 November 1962 — just 57 days after this marriage. The bride in the Couple relationship on this record is unambiguously identified as **Rosemarie Graham** (record persona ark:/61903/1:1:WBF8-1Q6Z; assertion a_070 — same source, primary information, direct evidence of the bride’s identity). No second wife named "Tossie Bain" exists in this record; the index title erroneously promoted the groom’s parent entry to the title slot.

**b. Corroboration from the Death Index**

The Oregon Death Index 1903–1998, a derivative state index compiled from underlying death certificates, records Benny Rapheal Taylor’s death on 9 July 1991 in Tillamook, Oregon, with surviving spouse listed as **“Rosema Taylor”** (assertions a_003, a_004, a_005; src_001 — "Oregon, Death Index, 1903–1998," FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:1:VZCQ-5XC; derivative record, secondary information about the spouse’s name, direct evidence of a surviving marital relationship). The name "Rosema" is a recognized index truncation of "Rosemarie." Combined with the 1962 Oregon marriage record identifying the bride as Rosemarie Graham, two independent sources separated by 29 years converge on the identification of **Rosemarie Graham** (tree: I1) as Benny’s fourth and final wife and surviving spouse. The original death certificate was not examined; the "Rosema" truncation is resolved by the independent marriage record.

The Oregon Marriage Index 1991–2006 (FamilySearch collection 3500591; log_003, negative) returned no record of any additional marriage by Benny after 1975, consistent with Rosemarie Graham remaining his wife until his death in 1991.

---

### Source Analysis and Confidence Tier

All five GPS components are addressed. Component 1 (reasonably exhaustive research): declared exhaustive per evaluation of log_001–log_009. Component 2 (complete and accurate citations): all assertions carry source citations with source classifications. Component 3 (analysis and correlation): assertions classified with information quality and evidence type; identity-linked via person_evidence. Component 4 (resolution of conflicting evidence): one apparent conflict ("Single" marital status in 1950 census) is resolved by the internal evidence of the same record; no unresolved conflicts exist. Component 5 (this reasoned written conclusion).

**Tier: Probable** — the evidence strongly preponderates in favor of four marriages with three named wives, but the GPS "proved" standard is not met because: (a) the Oregon marriage index entries used to document wives #2 and #4 (QDLH-77Y; the Tillamook 1941 marriage record for LL9V-56G) are derivative digital indexes rather than original certificate images examined in hand; (b) the Washington County marriage records for wife #3 are pre-existing tree sources not newly extracted as research.json assertions in this project — they are corroborated but not directly proved here; and (c) the unnamed first wife rests on a single source with no corroboration possible from available indexed records. The "proved" tier would require two independent original records with primary-quality information for each concluded marriage; only the census and draft card originals meet that standard, and they corroborate rather than directly document the marriages. A researcher examining the original Oregon marriage certificate images and the original death certificate would be positioned to elevate this conclusion to "proved."
